title: Graph DFS
description: DFS on the same adjacency list, side by side with Graph BFS on the exact same graph — proving the two really do explore differently.
objectives: Implement recursive DFS on a graph stored as an adjacency list
Trace DFS on the same graph used for Graph BFS
See the exploration order difference directly, side by side
Recognize this as the same DFS template applied to a new representation

sections.text: DFS was demonstrated on a grid. Here it is on the exact same graph used in Graph BFS, so the two exploration orders can be compared directly, side by side, on identical data.

sections.title: Tracing it, and comparing to Graph BFS's result
points: dfs(0): visit 0. First neighbor is 1 → commit fully to dfs(1) before ever looking at 0's second neighbor, 2
dfs(1): visit 1. First unvisited neighbor is 3 → commit fully to dfs(3)
dfs(3): visit 3. First unvisited neighbor is 2 → commit fully to dfs(2)
dfs(2): visit 2. Both neighbors (0, 3) already visited → return, backtrack to 3
Back in dfs(3): next neighbor is 4, unvisited → dfs(4). Visit 4, no unvisited neighbors → return
Backtrack all the way up: 3 done, 1 done, back to 0's second neighbor (2) — but 2 is ALREADY visited (from deep inside 3's exploration) → skip
Result: 0 1 3 2 4 — compare to Graph BFS's 0 1 2 3 4: BFS visited 2 right after 0 (breadth-first), DFS didn't reach 2 until diving through 1 and 3 first (depth-first)

sections.title: Exploring a friend network by following one thread of conversation to its end
sections.text: Recall DFS's maze-corridor analogy. On a social network, DFS is like following one friend's entire chain of connections as deep as it goes — their friend, that friend's friend, and so on — before ever circling back to your own second direct friend. BFS, by contrast, would check all of your direct friends first, together, before going any deeper.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the visited check before recursing — without it, a cyclic graph causes infinite recursion.
Assuming DFS and BFS visit vertices in the same order — as this trace shows directly, they genuinely differ, even on the identical graph and identical starting vertex.
Marking visited too late (after the recursive calls, instead of at the very start of the function) — allows a vertex to be entered multiple times before being marked.
Assuming the specific DFS order shown is the only valid one — the order in which adj[current]'s neighbors happen to be listed affects which specific order DFS explores them in.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does Graph DFS differ in implementation from Graph BFS?
questions.answer: DFS uses recursion (or an explicit stack), committing fully to one neighbor before trying the next; BFS uses a queue, processing neighbors in the order they were discovered, spreading outward level by level.

questions.question: On the same graph and starting vertex, do BFS and DFS necessarily visit vertices in the same order?
questions.answer: No — they commonly differ; BFS visits all direct neighbors before going deeper, while DFS dives as deep as possible down one path before backtracking.

questions.question: Why is the visited check essential in Graph DFS?
questions.answer: Because a graph can contain cycles, and without tracking visited vertices, DFS could recurse into an already-visited vertex indefinitely.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Graph DFS applies the same recursive template from DFS to an adjacency list: mark the current vertex visited, then recursively dive into each unvisited neighbor, committing fully to one before trying the next. Traced on the exact same graph as Graph BFS, the two produce genuinely different orders — 0 1 3 2 4 for DFS versus 0 1 2 3 4 for BFS — because DFS dives as deep as possible down the first available path before ever backtracking to try a vertex's other neighbors, while BFS processes all of a vertex's neighbors together before going any deeper.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Graph DFS
sections.text: Graph DFS = follow one friend's entire chain deep before checking your other direct friends. Compare directly to Graph BFS on the same graph — the orders genuinely differ.

sections.title: Number of Islands
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n binary grid where '1' is land and '0' is water, return the number of islands (groups of horizontally/vertically connected land).
hints: This is Graph DFS on an implicit grid graph — a cell's neighbors are its 4 grid directions instead of an explicit adjacency list.
Each time DFS starts fresh from an UNVISITED land cell, that's a brand new island — count starts, then DFS sinks the entire connected component.
Mark cells visited (or flip them to '0') during the DFS so the same land mass is never counted twice.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use a separate visited grid, DFS sinking each island; check the full grid.size()*grid[0].size() before the visited optimization below.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the separate visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sink islands in-place by flipping visited land to '0' — no separate visited grid needed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case recursion stack, O(1) extra grid space
